revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenson dynasty]][[ja:天孫氏]][[vi:Vương triều Thiên Tôn]][[zh:天孫氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q6146852]] Wikidata name: Tenson Dynasty [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The {{Nihongo|'''Tenson dynasty'''<ref>{{cite book| url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gvLGDwAAQBAJ&q=Tenson+Dynasty&pg=PA235 | title = Ancient Ryukyu: An Archaeological Study of Island Communities | author = Richard Pearson | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 2013 |isbn = 9780824865894 |quote = The victorious Ryukyu Kingdom compiled the ''Chūzan seikan'' as its history in the seventeenth century AD, outlining a succession of three early dynasties (Haneji 1983). These were the Tenson Dynasty, the Eiso Dynasty (AD 1260 to 1349), and the Satto Dynasty (AD 1350 to 1405).}}</ref>|天孫氏王統}} was the first dynasty in the traditional historiography of the [[:en:Ryukyu Islands|Ryukyu Islands]].<ref name="kerr"/> According to the Ryukyuan creation myth, the Heavenly Emperor (天帝), who lived in the Heavenly ''[[:en:Gusuku|Gusuku]]'' (天城), ordered [[Amamikyu]] to create the Ryukyu Islands. Without sexual intercourse, she became pregnant by a male deity, [[Shinerikyu]], and populated the islands.<ref name="glacken"/> Generations of mankind lived in caves and fields. At last there emerged a {{nihongo|"heavenly child"|天帝子|extra=''tenteishi''}}, who had three sons and two daughters:<ref name="kerr">Kerr. p36.</ref> the first son became the first king, the second son became the first lord (''[[:en:Aji (Ryukyu)|Aji]]''), the third son became the commoners; both daughters became the priestesses, the elder daughter became the first ''[[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]]'', while the younger became the first ''[[Noro (priestess)|noro]]''.<ref name="seikan"/><ref name="glacken">Glacken, Clarence. ''The Great Loochoo''. University of California Press, 1955. Pp 29-30.</ref> The ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'' claimed that the Tenson dynasty had twenty-five kings and ruled for seventeen thousand years. The last king was assassinated and usurped by a powerful official named [[Riyū]] (利勇), and later, Riyū was overthrown by [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], the lord of [[:en:Urasoe|Urasoe]], who established the Shunten dynasty in 1187.<ref name="seikan">''Chūzan Seikan'', [[:ja:s:琉球國中山世鑑/卷一|vol. 1]]</ref>
The ruler of the Tenson dynasty was only "one line of paramount local chieftains"<ref>Kerr. p44.</ref> during the [[:en:Gusuku period|Gusuku period]], and its royal authority was based on "divine right".<ref>Kerr. p45.</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * {{cite book |last=Kerr |first=George H. |author-link=George H. Kerr |date=October 2000 |title=Okinawa: The History of an Island People (revised ed.) |url= |location=Singapore |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |page=36 |isbn=978-0-8048-2087-5 }}
==See also== *[[Tenson kōrin]], a similar story in Japanese mythology
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Kings of Ryukyu}}]]
{{draft categories| [[Category:Asian dynasties]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]]
}}
[[en:Tenson dynasty]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenson dynasty]][[ja:天孫氏]][[vi:Vương triều Thiên Tôn]][[zh:天孫氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6146852]] Wikidata name: Tenson Dynasty [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The {{Nihongo|'''Tenson dynasty'''<ref>{{cite book| url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gvLGDwAAQBAJ&q=Tenson+Dynasty&pg=PA235 | title = Ancient Ryukyu: An Archaeological Study of Island Communities | author = Richard Pearson | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 2013 |isbn = 9780824865894 |quote = The victorious Ryukyu Kingdom compiled the ''Chūzan seikan'' as its history in the seventeenth century AD, outlining a succession of three early dynasties (Haneji 1983). These were the Tenson Dynasty, the Eiso Dynasty (AD 1260 to 1349), and the Satto Dynasty (AD 1350 to 1405).}}</ref>|天孫氏王統}} was the first dynasty in the traditional historiography of the [[:en:Ryukyu Islands|Ryukyu Islands]].<ref name="kerr"/> According to the Ryukyuan creation myth, the Heavenly Emperor (天帝), who lived in the Heavenly ''[[:en:Gusuku|Gusuku]]'' (天城), ordered [[Amamikyu]] to create the Ryukyu Islands. Without sexual intercourse, she became pregnant by a male deity, [[Shinerikyu]], and populated the islands.<ref name="glacken"/> Generations of mankind lived in caves and fields. At last there emerged a {{nihongo|"heavenly child"|天帝子|extra=''tenteishi''}}, who had three sons and two daughters:<ref name="kerr">Kerr. p36.</ref> the first son became the first king, the second son became the first lord (''[[:en:Aji (Ryukyu)|Aji]]''), the third son became the commoners; both daughters became the priestesses, the elder daughter became the first ''[[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]]'', while the younger became the first ''[[Noro (priestess)|noro]]''.<ref name="seikan"/><ref name="glacken">Glacken, Clarence. ''The Great Loochoo''. University of California Press, 1955. Pp 29-30.</ref> The ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'' claimed that the Tenson dynasty had twenty-five kings and ruled for seventeen thousand years. The last king was assassinated and usurped by a powerful official named [[Riyū]] (利勇), and later, Riyū was overthrown by [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], the lord of [[:en:Urasoe|Urasoe]], who established the Shunten dynasty in 1187.<ref name="seikan">''Chūzan Seikan'', [[:ja:s:琉球國中山世鑑/卷一|vol. 1]]</ref>
The ruler of the Tenson dynasty was only "one line of paramount local chieftains"<ref>Kerr. p44.</ref> during the [[:en:Gusuku period|Gusuku period]], and its royal authority was based on "divine right".<ref>Kerr. p45.</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * {{cite book |last=Kerr |first=George H. |author-link=George H. Kerr |date=October 2000 |title=Okinawa: The History of an Island People (revised ed.) |url= |location=Singapore |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |page=36 |isbn=978-0-8048-2087-5 }}
==See also== *[[Tenson kōrin]], a similar story in Japanese mythology

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenson dynasty]][[ja:天孫氏]][[vi:Vương triều Thiên Tôn]][[zh:天孫氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6146852]] Wikidata name: Tenson Dynasty [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The {{Nihongo|'''Tenson dynasty'''<ref>{{cite book| url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gvLGDwAAQBAJ&q=Tenson+Dynasty&pg=PA235 | title = Ancient Ryukyu: An Archaeological Study of Island Communities | author = Richard Pearson | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 2013 |isbn = 9780824865894 |quote = The victorious Ryukyu Kingdom compiled the ''Chūzan seikan'' as its history in the seventeenth century AD, outlining a succession of three early dynasties (Haneji 1983). These were the Tenson Dynasty, the Eiso Dynasty (AD 1260 to 1349), and the Satto Dynasty (AD 1350 to 1405).}}</ref>|天孫氏王統}} was the first dynasty in the traditional historiography of the [[:en:Ryukyu Islands|Ryukyu Islands]].<ref name="kerr"/> According to the Ryukyuan creation myth, the Heavenly Emperor (天帝), who lived in the Heavenly ''[[:en:Gusuku|Gusuku]]'' (天城), ordered [[Amamikyu]] to create the Ryukyu Islands. Without sexual intercourse, she became pregnant by a male deity, [[Shinerikyu]], and populated the islands.<ref name="glacken"/> Generations of mankind lived in caves and fields. At last there emerged a {{nihongo|"heavenly child"|天帝子|extra=''tenteishi''}}, who had three sons and two daughters:<ref name="kerr">Kerr. p36.</ref> the first son became the first king, the second son became the first lord (''[[:en:Aji (Ryukyu)|Aji]]''), the third son became the commoners; both daughters became the priestesses, the elder daughter became the first ''[[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]]'', while the younger became the first ''[[Noro (priestess)|noro]]''.<ref name="seikan"/><ref name="glacken">Glacken, Clarence. ''The Great Loochoo''. University of California Press, 1955. Pp 29-30.</ref> The ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'' claimed that the Tenson dynasty had twenty-five kings and ruled for seventeen thousand years. The last king was assassinated and usurped by a powerful official named [[Riyū]] (利勇), and later, Riyū was overthrown by [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], the lord of [[:en:Urasoe|Urasoe]], who established the Shunten dynasty in 1187.<ref name="seikan">''Chūzan Seikan'', [[:ja:s:琉球國中山世鑑/卷一|vol. 1]]</ref>
The ruler of the Tenson dynasty was only "one line of paramount local chieftains"<ref>Kerr. p44.</ref> during the [[:en:Gusuku period|Gusuku period]], and its royal authority was based on "divine right".<ref>Kerr. p45.</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * {{cite book |last=Kerr |first=George H. |author-link=George H. Kerr |date=October 2000 |title=Okinawa: The History of an Island People (revised ed.) |url= |location=Singapore |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |page=36 |isbn=978-0-8048-2087-5 }}
==See also== *[[Tenson kōrin]], a similar story in Japanese mythology
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Kings of Ryukyu}}]]

''The Great Loochoo''. University of California Press, 1955. ''The Great Loochoo''. University of California Press, 1955. Pp 29-30.</ref> The ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'' claimed that the Tenson dynasty had twenty-five kings and ruled for seventeen thousand years. The last king was assassinated and usurped by a powerful official named [[Riyū]] (利勇), and later, Riyū was overthrown by [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], the lord of [[:en:Urasoe|Urasoe]], who established the Shunten dynasty in 1187.<ref name="seikan">''Chūzan Seikan'', [[:ja:s:琉球國中山世鑑/卷一|vol. 1]]</ref>
The ruler of the Tenson dynasty was only "one line of paramount local chieftains"<ref>Kerr. p44.</ref> during the [[:en:Gusuku period|Gusuku period]], and its royal authority was based on "divine right".<ref>Kerr. p45.</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * {{cite book |last=Kerr |first=George H. |author-link=George H. Kerr |date=October 2000 |title=Okinawa: The History of an Island People (revised ed.) |url= |location=Singapore |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |page=36 |isbn=978-0-8048-2087-5 }}
==See also== *[[Tenson kōrin]], a similar story in Japanese mythology

These were the Tenson Dynasty, the Eiso Dynasty (AD 1260 to 1349), and the Satto Dynasty (AD 1350 to 1405).}}</ref>|天孫氏王統}} was the first dynasty in the traditional historiography of the [[:en:Ryukyu Islands|Ryukyu Islands]].<ref name="kerr"/> According to the Ryukyuan creation myth, the Heavenly Emperor (天帝), who lived in the Heavenly ''[[:en:Gusuku|Gusuku]]'' (天城), ordered [[Amamikyu]] to create the Ryukyu Islands. Without sexual intercourse, she became pregnant by a male deity, [[Shinerikyu]], and populated the islands.<ref name="glacken"/> Generations of mankind lived in caves and fields. At last there emerged a {{nihongo|"heavenly child"|天帝子|extra=''tenteishi''}}, who had three sons and two daughters:<ref name="kerr">Kerr. p36.</ref> the first son became the first king, the second son became the first lord (''[[:en:Aji (Ryukyu)|Aji]]''), the third son became the commoners; both daughters became the priestesses, the elder daughter became the first ''[[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]]'', while the younger became the first ''[[Noro (priestess)|noro]]''.<ref name="seikan"/><ref name="glacken">Glacken, Clarence. ''The Great Loochoo''. University of California Press, 1955. Pp 29-30.</ref> The ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'' claimed that the Tenson dynasty had twenty-five kings and ruled for seventeen thousand years. The last king was assassinated and usurped by a powerful official named [[Riyū]] (利勇), and later, Riyū was overthrown by [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], the lord of [[:en:Urasoe|Urasoe]], who established the Shunten dynasty in 1187.<ref name="seikan">''Chūzan Seikan'', [[:ja:s:琉球國中山世鑑/卷一|vol. 1]]</ref>
The ruler of the Tenson dynasty was only "one line of paramount local chieftains"<ref>Kerr. p44.</ref> during the [[:en:Gusuku period|Gusuku period]], and its royal authority was based on "divine right".<ref>Kerr. p45.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenson dynasty]][[ja:天孫氏]][[vi:Vương triều Thiên Tôn]][[zh:天孫氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenson dynasty]][[ja:天孫氏]][[vi:Vương triều Thiên Tôn]][[zh:天孫氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenson dynasty]][[ja:天孫氏]][[vi:Vương triều Thiên Tôn]][[zh:天孫氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6146852]] Wikidata name: Tenson Dynasty [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The {{Nihongo|'''Tenson dynasty'''<ref>{{cite book| url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gvLGDwAAQBAJ&q=Tenson+Dynasty&pg=PA235 | title = Ancient Ryukyu: An Archaeological Study of Island Communities | author = Richard Pearson | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 2013 |isbn = 9780824865894 |quote = The victorious Ryukyu Kingdom compiled the ''Chūzan seikan'' as its history in the seventeenth century AD, outlining a succession of three early dynasties (Haneji 1983). These were the Tenson Dynasty, the Eiso Dynasty (AD 1260 to 1349), and the Satto Dynasty (AD 1350 to 1405).}}</ref>|天孫氏王統}} was the first dynasty in the traditional historiography of the [[:en:Ryukyu Islands|Ryukyu Islands]].<ref name="kerr"/> According to the Ryukyuan creation myth, the Heavenly Emperor (天帝), who lived in the Heavenly ''[[:en:Gusuku|Gusuku]]'' (天城), ordered [[Amamikyu]] to create the Ryukyu Islands. Without sexual intercourse, she became pregnant by a male deity, [[Shinerikyu]], and populated the islands.<ref name="glacken"/> Generations of mankind lived in caves and fields. At last there emerged a {{nihongo|"heavenly child"|天帝子|extra=''tenteishi''}}, who had three sons and two daughters:<ref name="kerr">Kerr. p36.</ref> the first son became the first king, the second son became the first lord (''[[:en:Aji (Ryukyu)|Aji]]''), the third son became the commoners; both daughters became the priestesses, the elder daughter became the first ''[[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]]'', while the younger became the first ''[[Noro (priestess)|noro]]''.<ref name="seikan"/><ref name="glacken">Glacken, Clarence. ''The Great Loochoo''. University of California Press, 1955. Pp 29-30.</ref> The ''[[:en:Chūzan Seikan|Chūzan Seikan]]'' claimed that the Tenson dynasty had twenty-five kings and ruled for seventeen thousand years. The last king was assassinated and usurped by a powerful official named [[Riyū]] (利勇), and later, Riyū was overthrown by [[:en:Shunten|Shunten]], the lord of [[:en:Urasoe|Urasoe]], who established the Shunten dynasty in 1187.<ref name="seikan">''Chūzan Seikan'', [[:ja:s:琉球國中山世鑑/卷一|vol. 1]]</ref>
The ruler of the Tenson dynasty was only "one line of paramount local chieftains"<ref>Kerr. p44.</ref> during the [[:en:Gusuku period|Gusuku period]], and its royal authority was based on "divine right".<ref>Kerr. p45.</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * {{cite book |last=Kerr |first=George H. |author-link=George H. Kerr |date=October 2000 |title=Okinawa: The History of an Island People (revised ed.) |url= |location=Singapore |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |page=36 |isbn=978-0-8048-2087-5 }}
==See also== *[[Tenson kōrin]], a similar story in Japanese mythology
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Kings of Ryukyu}}]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenson dynasty]][[ja:天孫氏]][[vi:Vương triều Thiên Tôn]][[zh:天孫氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenson dynasty]][[ja:天孫氏]][[vi:Vương triều Thiên Tôn]][[zh:天孫氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tenson dynasty]][[ja:天孫氏]][[vi:Vương triều Thiên Tôn]][[zh:天孫氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6146852]] Wikidata name: Tenson Dynasty [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The {{Nihongo|'''Tenson dynasty'''<ref>{{cite book| url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gvLGDwAAQBAJ&q=Tenson+Dynasty&pg=PA235 | title = Ancient Ryukyu: An Archaeological Study of Island Communities | author = Richard Pearson | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 2013 |isbn = 9780824865894 |quote = The victorious Ryukyu Kingdom compiled the ''Chūzan seikan'' as its history in the seventeenth century AD, outlining a succession of three early dynasties (Haneji 1983). These were the Tenson Dynasty, the Eiso Dynasty (AD 1260 to 1349), and the Satto Dynasty (AD 1350 to 1405).}}</ref>|天孫氏王統}} was the first dynasty in the traditional historiography of the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|en|Ryukyu Islands|WD=Q6897416|lt=Ryukyu Islands}}.<ref name="kerr"/> According to the Ryukyuan creation myth, the Heavenly Emperor (天帝), who lived in the Heavenly ''{{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|WD=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku}}'' (天城), ordered [[Amamikyu]] to create the Ryukyu Islands. Without sexual intercourse, she became pregnant by a male deity, [[Shinerikyu]], and populated the islands.<ref name="glacken"/> Generations of mankind lived in caves and fields. At last there emerged a {{nihongo|"heavenly child"|天帝子|extra=''tenteishi''}}, who had three sons and two daughters:<ref name="kerr">Kerr. p36.</ref> the first son became the first king, the second son became the first lord (''{{ill|Aji (Ryukyu)|en|Aji (Ryukyu)|WD=Q1028297|lt=Aji}}''), the third son became the commoners; both daughters became the priestesses, the elder daughter became the first ''{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi}}'', while the younger became the first ''[[Noro (priestess)|noro]]''.<ref name="seikan"/><ref name="glacken">Glacken, Clarence. ''The Great Loochoo''. University of California Press, 1955. Pp 29-30.</ref> The ''{{ill|Chūzan Seikan|en|Chūzan Seikan|WD=Q8015936|lt=Chūzan Seikan}}'' claimed that the Tenson dynasty had twenty-five kings and ruled for seventeen thousand years. The last king was assassinated and usurped by a powerful official named [[Riyū]] (利勇), and later, Riyū was overthrown by {{ill|Shunten|en|Shunten|WD=Q1059918|lt=Shunten}}, the lord of [[:en:Urasoe|Urasoe]], who established the Shunten dynasty in 1187.<ref name="seikan">''Chūzan Seikan'', [[:ja:s:琉球國中山世鑑/卷一|vol. 1]]</ref>
The ruler of the Tenson dynasty was only "one line of paramount local chieftains"<ref>Kerr. p44.</ref> during the [[:en:Gusuku period|Gusuku period]], and its royal authority was based on "divine right".<ref>Kerr. p45.</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * {{cite book |last=Kerr |first=George H. |author-link=George H. Kerr |date=October 2000 |title=Okinawa: The History of an Island People (revised ed.) |url= |location=Singapore |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |page=36 |isbn=978-0-8048-2087-5 }}
==See also== *[[Tenson kōrin]], a similar story in Japanese mythology
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Kings of Ryukyu}}]]

These were the Tenson Dynasty, the Eiso Dynasty (AD 1260 to 1349), and the Satto Dynasty (AD 1350 to 1405).}}</ref>|天孫氏王統}} was the first dynasty in the traditional historiography of the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|en|Ryukyu Islands|WD=Q6897416|lt=Ryukyu Islands}}.<ref name="kerr"/> According to the Ryukyuan creation myth, the Heavenly Emperor (天帝), who lived in the Heavenly ''{{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|WD=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku}}'' (天城), ordered [[Amamikyu]] to create the Ryukyu Islands. Without sexual intercourse, she became pregnant by a male deity, [[Shinerikyu]], and populated the islands.<ref name="glacken"/> Generations of mankind lived in caves and fields. At last there emerged a {{nihongo|"heavenly child"|天帝子|extra=''tenteishi''}}, who had three sons and two daughters:<ref name="kerr">Kerr. p36.</ref> the first son became the first king, the second son became the first lord (''{{ill|Aji (Ryukyu)|en|Aji (Ryukyu)|WD=Q1028297|lt=Aji}}''), the third son became the commoners; both daughters became the priestesses, the elder daughter became the first ''{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi}}'', while the younger became the first ''[[Noro (priestess)|noro]]''.<ref name="seikan"/><ref name="glacken">Glacken, Clarence. ''The Great Loochoo''. University of California Press, 1955. Pp 29-30.</ref> The ''{{ill|Chūzan Seikan|en|Chūzan Seikan|WD=Q8015936|lt=Chūzan Seikan}}'' claimed that the Tenson dynasty had twenty-five kings and ruled for seventeen thousand years. The last king was assassinated and usurped by a powerful official named [[Riyū]] (利勇), and later, Riyū was overthrown by {{ill|Shunten|en|Shunten|WD=Q1059918|lt=Shunten}}, the lord of [[:en:Urasoe|Urasoe]], who established the Shunten dynasty in 1187.<ref name="seikan">''Chūzan Seikan'', [[:ja:s:琉球國中山世鑑/卷一|vol. 1]]</ref>
The ruler of the Tenson dynasty was only "one line of paramount local chieftains"<ref>Kerr. p44.</ref> during the [[:en:Gusuku period|Gusuku period]], and its royal authority was based on "divine right".<ref>Kerr. p45.</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * {{cite book |last=Kerr |first=George H. |author-link=George H. Kerr |date=October 2000 |title=Okinawa: The History of an Island People (revised ed.) |url= |location=Singapore |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |page=36 |isbn=978-0-8048-2087-5 }}
==See also== *[[Tenson kōrin]], a similar story in Japanese mythology

The last king was assassinated and usurped by a powerful official named [[Riyū]] (利勇), and later, Riyū was overthrown by {{ill|Shunten|en|Shunten|WD=Q1059918|lt=Shunten}}, the lord of [[:en:Urasoe|Urasoe]], who established the Shunten dynasty in 1187.<ref name="seikan">''Chūzan Seikan'', [[:ja:s:琉球國中山世鑑/卷一|vol. 1]]</ref>

The {{Nihongo|'''Tenson dynasty'''<ref>{{cite book| url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gvLGDwAAQBAJ&q=Tenson+Dynasty&pg=PA235 | title = Ancient Ryukyu: An Archaeological Study of Island Communities | author = Richard Pearson | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 2013 |isbn = 9780824865894 |quote = The victorious Ryukyu Kingdom compiled the ''Chūzan seikan'' as its history in the seventeenth century AD, outlining a succession of three early dynasties (Haneji 1983). These were the Tenson Dynasty, the Eiso Dynasty (AD 1260 to 1349), and the Satto Dynasty (AD 1350 to 1405).}}</ref>|天孫氏王統}} was the first dynasty in the traditional historiography of the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|en|Ryukyu Islands|WD=Q6897416|lt=Ryukyu Islands|1=Ryukyu Islands}}.<ref name="kerr"/> According to the Ryukyuan creation myth, the Heavenly Emperor (天帝), who lived in the Heavenly ''{{ill|Gusuku|en|Gusuku|WD=Q1044204|lt=Gusuku|1=Gusuku}}'' (天城), ordered [[Amamikyu]] to create the Ryukyu Islands. Without sexual intercourse, she became pregnant by a male deity, [[Shinerikyu]], and populated the islands.<ref name="glacken"/> Generations of mankind lived in caves and fields. At last there emerged a {{nihongo|"heavenly child"|天帝子|extra=''tenteishi''}}, who had three sons and two daughters:<ref name="kerr">Kerr. p36.</ref> the first son became the first king, the second son became the first lord (''{{ill|Aji (Ryukyu)|en|Aji (Ryukyu)|WD=Q1028297|lt=Aji|1=Aji (Ryukyu)}}''), the third son became the commoners; both daughters became the priestesses, the elder daughter became the first ''{{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|WD=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}}'', while the younger became the first ''[[Noro (priestess)|noro]]''.<ref name="seikan"/><ref name="glacken">Glacken, Clarence. ''The Great Loochoo''. University of California Press, 1955. Pp 29-30.</ref> The ''{{ill|Chūzan Seikan|en|Chūzan Seikan|WD=Q8015936|lt=Chūzan Seikan|1=Chūzan Seikan}}'' claimed that the Tenson dynasty had twenty-five kings and ruled for seventeen thousand years. The last king was assassinated and usurped by a powerful official named [[Riyū]] (利勇), and later, Riyū was overthrown by {{ill|Shunten|en|Shunten|WD=Q1059918|lt=Shunten|1=Shunten}}, the lord of [[:en:Urasoe|Urasoe]], who established the Shunten dynasty in 1187.<ref name="seikan">''Chūzan Seikan'', [[:ja:s:琉球國中山世鑑/卷一|vol. 1]]</ref>
The ruler of the Tenson dynasty was only "one line of paramount local chieftains"<ref>Kerr. p44.</ref> during the [[:en:Gusuku period|Gusuku period]], and its royal authority was based on "divine right".<ref>Kerr. p45.</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * {{cite book |last=Kerr |first=George H. |author-link=George H. Kerr |date=October 2000 |title=Okinawa: The History of an Island People (revised ed.) |url= |location=Singapore |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |page=36 |isbn=978-0-8048-2087-5 }}
==See also== *[[Tenson kōrin]], a similar story in Japanese mythology
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Kings of Ryukyu}}]]

The ruler of the Tenson dynasty was only "one line of paramount local chieftains"<ref>Kerr. p44.</ref> during the [[:en:Gusuku period|Gusuku period]], and its royal authority was based on "divine right".<ref>Kerr. p45.</ref>
